nytimes_articles.abstract:
Gov. George Pataki, appearing recently at a ceremony with Chief Judge Judith  S. Kaye of the New York State Court of Appeals, politely endorsed "judicial independence." Then he pursued his aggressive attack on judges who release dangerous criminals on bail. 

     That attack is politically promising. But it is also legally flawed.

History as Therapy: A Dangerous Idea
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Being a historian has almost become a dangerous occupation. The public seems  to have few inhibitions about passing judgment on the inner business of the historical community. This popular excitement over the use and abuse of history  can be both distracting and irritating for historians. But it is also a tribute  to the significance of history -- a tribute for which we should be grateful. It would be far worse if no one cared what we said or did. 

      So we inhabit embattled terrain. Consider the argument over national history standards. It would not seem unreasonable for historians to offer their informed judgment on the way history should be taught in our schools. Nor is there anything novel or revolutionary about such efforts.

The Invisible Supreme Court
nytimes_articles.abstract:
On April 24, the last oral arguments of the Supreme Court's term were watched by a crowded chamber of citizens, lawyers, guests and reporters. If the American people had seen the proceedings, they would have benefited. But because of the Justices' antagonism to televising the Court's public work, that day seems far off. 

     As Justice David Souter, who ordinarily shuns hyperbole, told a House subcommittee in March, "The day you see a camera coming into our courtroom, it's going to roll over my dead body."

nytimes_articles.headline: Corrections
Political Poison at the Grass Roots
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I know it is spring, not because of the burst of daffodils, the lengthening twilight or even the lime-green glow around the trees. I know it is spring because it is the season of the annual town meeting. The people here (pop. 9,000) gather to debate and approve the yearly budget. 

     These days, nothing about the process is appealing or uplifting. We do not sit as neighbors, questioning the merits of competing demands. We take on the demeanor of Newt Gingrich and pit bulls.

nytimes_articles.headline: Preserving History -- Natural and Otherwise
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Lured by the postcard images etched in their minds, millions of vacationers will soon head to our national parks. Everybody loves the parks. They are the envy of the world, one of America's greatest treasures, home to some precious elements of our heritage. Sadly, they are also a testament to the erosion of our national resolve to keep that heritage alive. 

     The national park system is much more than geysers, giant redwoods and grizzly bears. More than half of the 368 sites that make up the system are historic places outside of the great parks. Altogether, the National Park Service oversees more than 20,000 structures -- everything from Independence Hall in Philadelphia to, say, an early barn in Wyoming.
